replies.author: @nevergivint0tr011s-k2u
replies.text: @The_Reality_Filter Two 9@y people having a child and being forced to have their child undergo _gender_re-assignment_surgery and actually going into a court battle only to lose the case. That is not Star Trek and that is not how Star Trek would've handled that situation. I seem to recall what Picard said when a Starfleet Admiral wanted Data to give up his child for Starfleet research. He said, "Order a man to give up his child to the state? Not while I'm his captain." And then there was that time when the Klingon Empire ordered the death of Kurn and Worf for their father being a traitor when it turned out their father wasn't a traitor after all and Picard intervened pretty heavily here but was ordered by Worf to back off because Worf himself consented to take the blame with the public display to point out Duras as the real son of a traitor. Worf on his own volition and not the government's chose to take the wrap so that the Klingon Empire would remain intact and not go into battle with the Federation. I don't seem to recall either parent on Orville giving their consent to have their child mutilated by the government and yes it is mutilation. To borrow a quote from another classic TV show, "cutting into a healthy body is mutilation!" These Starfleet wannabes didn't even have the backbone or the legal knowledge to stand up to that government and fight for that crewman's rights and that crewman didn't have the backbone to stand up for his own rights. No it was a simple case of what the government says is what you're gonna do. The government is always right and the government always wins ,Bullshyt! If you think that's what Star Trek is then you're no more knowledgable about Star Trek than Alex Kurtzman or the brainwashed minions doing his bidding. You call yourself the Reality filter. What you are is what's left over when all reality is filtered out, The Bullshyt.

text: They apparently made 2 seasons. There is precedent for shows to be cancelled despite completed seasons (Star Trek Prodigy, for a relevant example, and of course Batgirl stands as an example of an entire film being junked) but the odds are slim. This is Star Trek and there is still a market for it. Though if the ratings (and show) don't improve - it's unlikely but not impossible: TNG was absolutely terrible for most of its first season though the ratings were good - I'd think that second season might get burned off in some way.

author: @henryanderson9502
text:
​"I've seen every Star Trek multiple times; I'd say probably ten to fifteen plus on most episodes. I am, at heart and soul, a Star Trek fan—may the Great Bird of the Galaxy strike me down. I've always thought that Star Trek in itself is an interpretive medium to discuss the flaws in our society, however they may arise.
​In a sense, it is a mirror to what goes on in the world; how we look at it is basically just how we see the world. Of course, everyone's interpretation is going to be different because everyone sees the world differently, but everyone's actually just looking at the same problems that we see every day, just dressed up in a different way.
​I think there's nothing wrong with that. I think all Star Trek conveys that in its own way, which makes sense; if you're conveying different concepts, you'd have to change the way you look. But at the core, Star Trek still does the same thing: holds up a mirror and shows us who we are."

replies.text: it's a number of reasons, the show was greenlit before SkyDance took over Paramount, & they need to now try to get at some of their money back, as it's more than likely they already have several episodes produced with each one projected to cost between $7 to $10 million each to make, plus right now it doesn't look good to be canceling shows when Skydance is also trying to do the hostile takeover of WB Discovery from Netflix which might be facing monopoly antitrust, and not be approved by the gov, so they need to do all they can during this time to convince other stockholders to side with them as SkyDance only has a 20% stake in WB Discovery, which is not enough to force the hostile takeover, and not some other company if the Netflix deal falls through.

author: @rossarama
text: Classic Star Trek (pre-2000s) was aspirational- what was defined as aspirational for everybody. And it was intelligent, thoughtful stuff about the nature of humanity, time travel paradoxes, reality and so forth. The present stuff is aspirational as defined by a small insular echo chamber Hollywood elite; and its stupid and vapid and doesn't encourage any consideration of the human condition or the nature of reality etc etc.Thats why it sucks and doesn't get broad appeal

author: @citizensnips7949
text:
What I don't understand is the presence of fat people and crippled people? They established in TNG that replicators produce food with ideal nutrition. This is why Troi can binge on ice cream nightly without gaining weight. They literally had that tech 800 years before Starfleet Academy. Same with the cripples? They had the technology to outright cure the majority of the problems that would cause you to be a cripple, 800 years before STA.

A good future isn't one where there are ample wheelchair ramps everywhere. A good future is one where wheelchairs are obsolete because every problem that could put you in one has been medically solved.

Again, the people that are all about that shit have ZERO awareness of how unpopular their movement is.  None.

author: @gameburn178
text: If it were a cartoon, and it was released for the Saturday morning crowd of kids in 1980, maybe there would be an audience? To be fair, I don't listen to Taylor Swift -- but there is certainly an audience for her work. Maybe the audience is children, say 6 to 11 year olds? More for girls than boys?  It cannot possibly be for the fans of Next Gen or the Original Series, or Voyager and Enterprise. And yet there is swearing and odd morality in it -- cheating, for example, and excuse-making for criminals. So,... not for kids either?  No apparent audience I can see...

author: @John..219
text: "...while defenders highlight Star Trek's tradition of inlcusion ......" Yes, but with common sense. There are no obese people in the future of Star Trek as we would cured obesity within this century (Ozempic???), there are no people who needs glasses as Geordi La'forge was born blind and he could see with implants, there are no people in wheelchairs as Worf could grown a new spine after an injury. In DS9 Dr. Bashir, could decode so many deseases and even cured a plaque created by the Dominion to eradicate a whole planet and then, you couldn't cure obesity, shortsighted or paralysis? Give me a break. Inclusion? Yes, please but with common sense.
